# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ — Introduction

Welcome back! In Module 1, you explored the full Ukrainian alphabet — all 33 letters — and practiced reading your first words. You learned that Ukrainian has two types of sounds: vowels and consonants. Today, you're going to master the complete vowel system.

Here's a powerful rule that will help you read any Ukrainian word: every syllable has exactly one vowel. Count the vowels, and you know how many syllables the word has. Take **молоко́** (milk) — three vowels, three syllables. That simple.

Ukrainian has 10 vowel letters in total: six base vowels and four special «double-duty» vowels called iotated vowels. By the end of this module, you'll be able to pronounce and recognize every one of them. Let's get started!

---

## Шість основних голосних — Six Base Vowels

These six vowels are the foundation of Ukrainian pronunciation. Each one has a clear, steady sound that never changes — no matter where it appears in a word.

### Літера А

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — А](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hvB3VpcR3ZE)

**А** sounds like the «a» in «father» — open, clear, and steady. It never weakens or changes. You already know **ма́ма** (mom) from Module 1. Now meet two more:

- **ка́ша** — porridge (a classic Ukrainian breakfast)
- **са́ло** — cured pork fat (a beloved Ukrainian food)

Say each word aloud. Feel how the **А** stays exactly the same every time.

### Літера О

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — О](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gJFxRIPRZbI)

**О** sounds like the «o» in «more» — rounded and full. Here's something important: Ukrainian **О** keeps its pure sound even when it's not stressed. It never fades into «uh.»

- **о́ко** — eye (M1 review)
- **молоко́** — milk (M1 review — three О sounds, all identical!)
- **село́** — village

Listen carefully to **село́** — the stress falls on the last syllable, but the first **Е** stays clear too. You'll learn more about stress soon.

### Літера У

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — У](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VB1O6PmtYRU)

**У** sounds like «oo» in «moon» — deep and rounded.

- **тут** — here (M1 review)
- **ву́хо** — ear
- **суп** — soup

### Літера Е

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Е](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KFlsroBW0dk)

**Е** sounds like the «e» in «set.» Be careful — this is NOT the English «ee» sound. Keep it short and open.

- **не́бо** — sky
- **село́** — village (notice **Е** stays clear even when unstressed)
- **день** — day

Don't worry if you see unfamiliar consonant letters like **Д**, **В**, or **Р** in these examples. Focus on the vowel sound — you'll master all the consonants in Module 3.

### Літера И

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — И](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W-1rCu0indE)

**И** is uniquely Ukrainian — there is no exact match in English. To make this sound, relax your jaw and let your tongue sit lower than for **І**. Think of a sound somewhere between «i» in «bit» and «e» in «bet.»

- **ри́ба** — fish
- **сир** — cheese
- **син** — son

### Літера І

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — І](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9TH0H4ShGo)

**І** sounds like «ee» in «see» — bright, high, and tense. Compare it to **И**: your tongue is higher and your lips spread wider for **І**.

- **ліс** — forest (M1 review)
- **кіт** — cat (M1 review)
- **сік** — juice (M1 review)

> [!tip] **The Big Challenge: И vs І**
> This is the hardest vowel contrast for English speakers. Try this minimal pair: **кит** (whale) vs **кіт** (cat). For **кит**, relax your jaw and lower your tongue. For **кіт**, raise your tongue and spread your lips. Practice switching back and forth — feel the difference in your jaw position!

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 2, p.10 -->

---

## Наголос — Word Stress

Every Ukrainian word has one stressed syllable. The stressed vowel is louder and slightly longer — but its quality does NOT change. This is the most important thing to remember:

> [!warning] **Golden Rule: Ukrainian vowels stay pure**
> Stressed or unstressed, every vowel keeps its full, clear sound. Never swallow an unstressed vowel into a lazy «uh» sound. This is non-negotiable!

This is very different from English. Think about how you say «photograph» versus «photography» — the vowels shift and blur depending on stress. In Ukrainian, that never happens.

Take **молоко́** (milk). The stress falls on the last syllable: мо-ло-КО́. But all three **О** sounds are exactly the same — pure, rounded **О**. Your English habits will try to say something like «muh-luh-KO.» Fight that urge! Give every **О** its full, clear sound.

Another example: **село́** (village). Stress on the last syllable, but the **Е** in the first syllable stays just as clear as in **не́бо** (sky), where **Е** is stressed.

Take your time with this. Keeping vowels pure is a skill that takes practice, and you're building it right now.

<!-- adapted from: Большакова, Grade 2, p.37 -->

---

## Йотовані голосні — Iotated Vowels

Now for something special. Four Ukrainian vowel letters — **Я**, **Ю**, **Є**, **Ї** — are «double-duty» letters. At the start of a word or after another vowel, each one represents TWO sounds: the semi-vowel **Й** plus a base vowel. After a consonant, **Я**, **Ю**, and **Є** have a different job — they soften that consonant instead.

<!-- adapted from: Кравцова, Grade 2, p.10 — exercise 30 -->

### Літера Я

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Я](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yhSAf41LX8I)

**Я** = **Й** + **А** (two sounds at word start or after a vowel).

- At the start of a word: **я́блуко** (apple). You hear both sounds — «YAH-blu-ko.» You'll also find **Я** at the start of **яйце́** (egg).
- After a vowel: **моя́** (my, for feminine nouns) — «mo-YAH.» Again, two sounds.
- After a consonant: **дя́дько** (uncle) — here **Я** softens the **Д** instead. Only one vowel sound.

### Літера Ю

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ю](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9JdIBYCTWGw)

**Ю** = **Й** + **У** (two sounds at word start or after a vowel).

- At the start: **юна́к** (young man), **ю́шка** (soup, broth).
- After a consonant: **лю́ди** (people) — the **Л** becomes soft. Only one vowel sound.

### Літера Є

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Є](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0bwRyyBQSc)

**Є** = **Й** + **Е** (two sounds at word start or after a vowel).

- At the start: **Євро́па** (Europe).
- After a vowel: **моє́** (my, for neuter nouns) — «mo-YE.» Two sounds.
- After a consonant: **си́нє** (blue, neuter) — the **Н** becomes soft. One vowel sound.

### Літера Ї

[Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ї](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UcjdjQXhAY8)

**Ї** is the simplest iotated vowel — it ALWAYS represents two sounds: **Й** + **І**. It never softens a consonant, and it never reduces to one sound. When you see **Ї**, you always hear both parts.

- **їжа́к** — hedgehog
- **їжа́** — food
- **Украї́на** — Ukraine

> [!culture] **Ї — A Symbol of Ukrainian Identity**
> The letter **Ї** exists only in the Ukrainian alphabet. No other Slavic language has it. For many Ukrainians, **Ї** is a proud symbol of their unique linguistic identity. When you pronounce it, you're making a sound that belongs only to Ukrainian.

### Літера Й

**Й** is the semi-vowel itself — a short, quick sound. It appears in words like:

- **край** — edge, land, homeland
- **йо́гурт** — yogurt

Remember: **Й** never forms a syllable on its own. It always pairs with a vowel next to it.

---

## Голосні в словах — Vowels in Words

Let's put your vowel knowledge to work! Here are short sentences using words you know. Read them aloud and pay attention to every vowel. Don't worry about consonants you haven't formally studied — focus on the vowels.

> — Це я́блуко? (Is this an apple?)
> — Так, це я́блуко. (Yes, this is an apple.)

> — Молоко́ тут? (Is the milk here?)
> — Ні, молоко́ там. (No, the milk is there.)

> — Це моє́ село́. (This is my village.)
> — А це мій край. (And this is my land.)

> — Де мій кіт? (Where is my cat?)
> — Кіт тут! (The cat is here!)

> — Це моя́ ка́ша. (This is my porridge.)
> — А це мій суп. (And this is my soup.)

<!-- adapted from: Захарійчук, Grade 1 -->

> [!practice] **Count the Vowels**
> Every vowel = one syllable. Try counting:
> - **кіт** — 1 vowel (І) = 1 syllable
> - **ри́ба** — 2 vowels (И, А) = 2 syllables
> - **молоко́** — 3 vowels (О, О, О) = 3 syllables
> - **я́блуко** — 3 vowels (Я, У, О) = 3 syllables
> - **Украї́на** — 4 vowels (У, А, Ї, А) = 4 syllables

You're doing great! You can now read the heartbeat of any Ukrainian word.

---

## Підсумок — Summary

You've just learned the complete Ukrainian vowel system! Here's what you now know:

- **10 vowel letters**: 6 base (**А О У Е И І**) + 4 iotated (**Я Ю Є Ї**) + the semi-vowel **Й**
- **The Golden Rule**: Ukrainian vowels stay pure — stressed or unstressed, they never reduce or blur. Fight the English instinct to swallow unstressed vowels!
- **Iotated vowels** do double duty: two sounds at word start or after a vowel, one sound (softening) after a consonant. **Ї** always stays two sounds.

**Self-check** — can you answer these?

1. Can you pronounce all six base vowels? Say them: А, О, У, Е, И, І.
2. What two sounds does **Я** make at the start of a word?
3. What is the difference between **И** and **І**? Which one is higher?
4. How many syllables does **молоко́** have? How do you know?

**Coming up next:** Module 3 takes you into the consonant system — voiced and voiceless pairs, sonorant consonants, and the difference between hard and soft. See you there!
```

---

module: a1-002
level: A1
sequence: 2
slug: vowel-sounds
version: '2.3'
title: Vowel Sounds
subtitle: "Голосні звуки — The Heartbeat of Every Ukrainian Word"
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Learner masters pronunciation of 6 base vowels (А О У Е И І)
- Learner understands iotated vowels (Я Ю Є Ї) and their dual function
- Learner understands Й as a semi-vowel
- Learner can read words containing all 10 vowel letters
- "Golden Rule: Ukrainian vowels stay pure — never reduce like English or Russian"
content_outline:
- section: Вступ — Introduction
  words: 100
  points:
  - "Review: M1 gave you the alphabet map and 10 practice letters. Today: the vowel
    system — 10 letters that carry every Ukrainian syllable."
  - "Why vowels matter: every syllable has exactly one vowel. Count the vowels
    and you know how many syllables a word has."
- section: "Шість основних голосних — Six Base Vowels"
  words: 300
  points:
  - "А — open, like 'a' in 'father'. Never reduces. Words: мама (M1 review),
    каша (porridge), сало (lard)."
  - "О — rounded, like 'o' in 'more'. Stays О even when unstressed (unlike Russian!).
    Words: око (M1 review), молоко (M1 review), село (village)."
  - "У — like 'oo' in 'moon'. Words: тут (M1 review), вухо (ear), суп (soup)."
  - "Е — like 'e' in 'set'. NOT like English 'ee'. Words: небо (sky), село (village).
    New consonants Д, В, Р appear in examples — focus is on the vowel sound."
  - "И — uniquely Ukrainian. No exact English equivalent. Jaw relaxed, tongue lower
    than І. Words: риба (fish), сир (cheese), син (son)."
  - "І — like 'ee' in 'see'. Brighter and higher than И. Words: ліс (M1 review),
    кіт (M1 review), сік (M1 review)."
  - "The И vs І distinction is the hardest vowel contrast for English speakers.
    Drill with minimal pairs: кит (whale) vs кіт (cat). Feel the jaw position."
- section: "Наголос — Word Stress"
  words: 150
  points:
  - "Every Ukrainian word has one stressed syllable. The stressed vowel is louder
    and slightly longer, but its quality does NOT change."
  - "Golden Rule: Ukrainian vowels stay pure in any position — stressed or unstressed.
    English speakers naturally swallow unstressed vowels into schwa (uh). Fight this!"
  - "Example: молоко — stress on last syllable (молокО), but all three О's sound
    the same. Compare English 'photograph' where vowels shift with stress."
- section: "Йотовані голосні — Iotated Vowels"
  words: 350
  points:
  - "Я, Ю, Є, Ї are 'double-duty' vowels. At word start or after another vowel,
    they represent TWO sounds: Й + base vowel."
  - "Я = й+а. At start: яблуко (apple). After vowel: моя (my-f).
    After consonant: softens it (дядько — Д becomes soft before Я)."
  - "Ю = й+у. At start: юнак (young man), юшка (broth). After consonant: softens it
    (люди — Л becomes soft)."
  - "Є = й+е. At start: Європа (Europe). After vowel: моє (my-n)."
  - "Ї = ALWAYS two sounds й+і, never softens a consonant. Words: їжак (hedgehog),
    їжа (food), Україна. Cultural note: the letter Ї as a symbol of Ukrainian identity."
  - "Й — the semi-vowel itself. Short consonant-like sound. Words: край (edge/land),
    йогурт. Never forms a syllable alone."
- section: "Голосні в словах — Vowels in Words"
  words: 200
  points:
  - "Reading practice — words organized by vowel focus. Use any consonants freely
    (consonant system is covered in M3)."
  - "Short sentences: Це яблуко. Це моє село. Мама каже 'так'. Де мій кіт?"
  - "Count-the-vowels exercise: молоко (3 vowels = 3 syllables), Україна (5 vowels
    = 5 syllables), кіт (1 vowel = 1 syllable)."
- section: "Підсумок — Summary"
  words: 100
  points:
  - "10 vowel letters: 6 base (А О У Е И І) + 4 iotated (Я Ю Є Ї) + semi-vowel Й."
  - "Golden Rule reinforced: Ukrainian vowels stay pure — never swallow or reduce them."
  - "Self-check: Can you pronounce all 6 base vowels? What two sounds does Я make
    at word start? What is the difference between И and І?"
  - "Next: M3 masters the consonant system — voiced/voiceless pairs, sonorants,
    hard vs soft."

  - "яблуко (apple) — key word for Я; Bolshakova p.18"
  - "риба (fish) — key word for И; high-frequency; Bolshakova"
  - "село (village) — demonstrates unstressed Е staying pure; high-frequency"

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: watch-and-repeat
  title: "Vowel Pronunciation Drill"
  instruction: "Watch each video, then repeat the vowel sound and example word aloud. Focus on keeping each vowel pure and clear."
  items:
    - letter: "А"
      word: "мама"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hvB3VpcR3ZE"
      note: "Open sound like 'a' in 'father'"
    - letter: "О"
      word: "око"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gJFxRIPRZbI"
      note: "Rounded sound like 'o' in 'more' — stays pure even unstressed"
    - letter: "У"
      word: "тут"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VB1O6PmtYRU"
      note: "Deep sound like 'oo' in 'moon'"
    - letter: "Е"
      word: "небо"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KFlsroBW0dk"
      note: "Like 'e' in 'set' — NOT English 'ee'"
    - letter: "И"
      word: "риба"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W-1rCu0indE"
      note: "Uniquely Ukrainian — jaw relaxed, tongue lower than for І"
    - letter: "І"
      word: "кіт"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z9TH0H4ShGo"
      note: "Like 'ee' in 'see' — tongue high, lips spread"
    - letter: "Я"
      word: "яблуко"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yhSAf41LX8I"
      note: "At word start = Й + А (two sounds)"
    - letter: "Ю"
      word: "юнак"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9JdIBYCTWGw"
      note: "At word start = Й + У (two sounds)"
    - letter: "Є"
      word: "Європа"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0bwRyyBQSc"
      note: "At word start = Й + Е (two sounds)"
    - letter: "Ї"
      word: "їжак"
      video: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UcjdjQXhAY8"
      note: "ALWAYS two sounds Й + І — unique to Ukrainian"

- type: classify
  title: "Sort the Vowel Letters"
  instruction: "Drag each letter into the correct category. Base vowels have one simple sound. Iotated vowels can represent two sounds."
  categories:
    - label: "Base Vowels"
      symbol_hint: "vowel"
      items: ["А", "О", "У", "Е", "И", "І"]
    - label: "Iotated Vowels"
      symbol_hint: "vowel"
      items: ["Я", "Ю", "Є", "Ї"]

- type: image-to-letter
  title: "What Letter Does It Start With?"
  instruction: "Look at the picture and tap the Ukrainian vowel letter the word starts with."
  items:
    - emoji: "🍎"
      answer: "Я"
      distractors: ["А", "Ю"]
      note: "яблуко (apple)"
    - emoji: "🦔"
      answer: "Ї"
      distractors: ["І", "Й"]
      note: "їжак (hedgehog)"
    - emoji: "👁️"
      answer: "О"
      distractors: ["А", "У"]
      note: "око (eye)"
    - emoji: "🥚"
      answer: "Я"
      distractors: ["Є", "А"]
      note: "яйце (egg)"
    - emoji: "🇺🇦"
      answer: "У"
      distractors: ["Ю", "О"]
      note: "Україна (Ukraine)"
    - emoji: "🌍"
      answer: "Є"
      distractors: ["Е", "Ю"]
      note: "Європа (Europe)"
    - emoji: "👨"
      answer: "Ю"
      distractors: ["У", "Я"]
      note: "юнак (young man)"
    - emoji: "🍽️"
      answer: "Ї"
      distractors: ["І", "Є"]
      note: "їжа (food)"

- type: quiz
  title: "И vs І — Spot the Difference"
  instruction: "These two vowels are the hardest contrast for English speakers. Test your knowledge!"
  items:
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word риба (fish)?"
      explanation: "Риба contains И — the uniquely Ukrainian vowel with a relaxed jaw position."
      options:
        - text: "И"
          correct: true
        - text: "І"
          correct: false
        - text: "Е"
          correct: false
        - text: "А"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word кіт (cat)?"
      explanation: "Кіт contains І — the high, bright vowel like 'ee' in 'see'."
      options:
        - text: "І"
          correct: true
        - text: "И"
          correct: false
        - text: "У"
          correct: false
        - text: "О"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word сир (cheese)?"
      explanation: "Сир contains И — tongue lower, jaw more relaxed than for І."
      options:
        - text: "И"
          correct: true
        - text: "І"
          correct: false
        - text: "Е"
          correct: false
        - text: "А"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word ліс (forest)?"
      explanation: "Ліс contains І — tongue high, lips spread wide."
      options:
        - text: "І"
          correct: true
        - text: "И"
          correct: false
        - text: "Е"
          correct: false
        - text: "О"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word син (son)?"
      explanation: "Син contains И — the distinctly Ukrainian vowel between English 'i' in 'bit' and 'e' in 'bet'."
      options:
        - text: "И"
          correct: true
        - text: "І"
          correct: false
        - text: "А"
          correct: false
        - text: "У"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel appears in the word сік (juice)?"
      explanation: "Сік contains І — bright and high, like 'ee' in 'see'."
      options:
        - text: "І"
          correct: true
        - text: "И"
          correct: false
        - text: "Е"
          correct: false
        - text: "О"
          correct: false
    - question: "The words кит (whale) and кіт (cat) differ by one vowel. Which word contains И?"
      explanation: "Кит (whale) has И (relaxed jaw). Кіт (cat) has І (tongue high). This minimal pair is the key drill!"
      options:
        - text: "кит (whale)"
          correct: true
        - text: "кіт (cat)"
          correct: false
        - text: "Both words"
          correct: false
        - text: "Neither word"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which description matches the vowel І?"
      explanation: "І is high and bright — tongue raised, lips spread. И is the opposite: jaw relaxed, tongue low."
      options:
        - text: "High, bright, tongue raised, lips spread"
          correct: true
        - text: "Relaxed jaw, tongue lower"
          correct: false
        - text: "Rounded, like 'oo' in 'moon'"
          correct: false
        - text: "Open, like 'a' in 'father'"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel is uniquely Ukrainian — no exact match in English?"
      explanation: "И has no exact English equivalent. It sits between 'i' in 'bit' and 'e' in 'bet'."
      options:
        - text: "И"
          correct: true
        - text: "І"
          correct: false
        - text: "А"
          correct: false
        - text: "О"
          correct: false
    - question: "Which vowel sounds like 'ee' in the English word 'see'?"
      explanation: "І sounds like English 'ee' — bright, high, and tense."
      options:
        - text: "І"
          correct: true
        - text: "И"
          correct: false
        - text: "Е"
          correct: false
        - text: "У"
          correct: false

- type: group-sort
  title: "Iotated Vowels — One Sound or Two?"
  instruction: "Sort each word by whether its iotated vowel (Я, Ю, Є, or Ї) represents one sound or two sounds in that word."
  groups:
    - name: "Two sounds (at word start or after a vowel)"
      items:
        - "яблуко"
        - "юнак"
        - "їжак"
        - "моя"
        - "моє"
        - "їжа"
        - "яйце"
    - name: "One sound (softens the consonant before it)"
      items:
        - "дядько"
        - "люди"
        - "синє"

- type: fill-in
  title: "Count the Vowels, Count the Syllables"
  instruction: "Every vowel = one syllable. Count the vowels in each word and pick the correct number of syllables."
  items:
    - sentence: "молоко has ___ syllables."
      answer: "3"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "мо-ло-ко — three vowels (О, О, О), three syllables."
    - sentence: "кіт has ___ syllable(s)."
      answer: "1"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "кіт — one vowel (І), one syllable."
    - sentence: "риба has ___ syllables."
      answer: "2"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "ри-ба — two vowels (И, А), two syllables."
    - sentence: "яблуко has ___ syllables."
      answer: "3"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "я-блу-ко — three vowels (Я, У, О), three syllables."
    - sentence: "Україна has ___ syllables."
      answer: "4"
      options: ["2", "3", "4", "5"]
      explanation: "У-кра-ї-на — four vowels (У, А, Ї, А), four syllables."
    - sentence: "село has ___ syllable(s)."
      answer: "2"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "се-ло — two vowels (Е, О), two syllables."
    - sentence: "мама has ___ syllables."
      answer: "2"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "ма-ма — two vowels (А, А), two syllables."
    - sentence: "небо has ___ syllables."
      answer: "2"
      options: ["1", "2", "3", "4"]
      explanation: "не-бо — two vowels (Е, О), two syllables."
```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Vowel Sounds

## State Standard Reference
§4.1.4: "Vowel and consonant sounds" (lines 577–584)
§4.1.5: "Word stress" (line 586)
Alignment: This module directly addresses §4.1.4 (vowel sound inventory — 6 base + 4 iotated) and §4.1.5 (наголос and the vowel purity rule). Both gates must pass for A1 phonetics compliance.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| яблуко | High-frequency; opener word for Я; children's lit staple | червоне яблуко |
| риба | Top-200 noun; canonical И-word; food vocab | смачна риба |
| село | Top-100; demonstrates unstressed Е staying pure (сеЛО not "suhLO") | моє село |
| Україна | Cultural anchor; 5-vowel word, perfect syllable-count demo | — |
| їжак | Children's lit staple (Їжачок); perfect Ї-opener word | — |
| юнак | State Standard vocab; clean Ю-at-start; folk register | молодий юнак |
| край | Top-100; semi-vowel Й at coda; poetic range (homeland) | мій край |
| день | Top-50; Е + soft Н; temporal anchor word | кожний день |
| син | High-frequency family term; clean И demonstration | мій син |
| моя / моє | Sight-word possessives; show Я/Є after vowel (dual-sound context) | моя мама, моє небо |
| вухо | Body vocab; clean У; useful for ear-body connection | — |
| їжа | Everyday food; Ї always two sounds | смачна їжа |
| яйце | Everyday food; Я at word start | — |
| юшка | Everyday soup/broth; Ю at word start | — |
| каша | Common food; clean А; Bolshakova p.18 | смачна каша |
| небо | Top-100; Е in open syllable; poetic | синє небо |
| сир | Food; И-word; minimal pair anchor (сир / сір) | — |

All 18 words VESUM-verified (18/18 found).

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Їжачок folk character** — їжак (hedgehog) appears in Ukrainian folk riddles and children's verse. Classic загадка: *«Не собака, а кусає?»* — їжак. Perfect Ї hook with cultural warmth.
2. **Каша as national food identity** — каша (porridge) is the quintessential Ukrainian comfort food, referenced in proverbs (*«Щи та каша — їжа наша»* — though the proverb is shared, каша is a real daily staple). Grounds А in daily life.
3. **Україна as 5-vowel showcase** — У-К-Р-А-Ї-Н-А: a 4-syllable word that contains А, Ї, and У — the country name itself teaches vowel counting. Deeply motivating for learners to count the heartbeat of their target language's name.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Vowel reduction on unstressed syllables** → Pronounce all О's in молокО as the same pure /o/, not /ə/. English speakers default to schwa on unstressed syllables ("muhluhKO"). Textbook exercises target this with multi-syllable repetition drills (cf. kravtsova grade 3: наголос section).
2. **И pronounced as "ee" (І)** → Correct form: jaw relaxed, tongue body lower than for І. Minimal pair: **сир** /sɪr/ (cheese) vs **сір** /sir/ (grey) — feeling the difference is key. English has no equivalent phoneme.
3. **Iotated vowels treated as single sounds at word start** → Learners read Я as just /a/ instead of /ja/. Contrast: **мати** (mother) vs **м'яти** (to crumple) — without the /j/ onset, meaning collapses.
4. **Ї conflated with Є or І** → Ї is ALWAYS two sounds /ji/ — it never softens a preceding consonant (unlike Я/Ю/Є). Drills using їжак, їжа, Україна reinforce the invariant two-sound rule.

---

## Cross-References

- **Builds on**: a1-001 (The Ukrainian Alphabet) — M1 introduced letter shapes and 10 practice letters; this module builds the phonological system on that foundation
- **Prepares for**: a1-003 (Consonant Sounds) — vowel purity rule recurs when consonants soften; И/І distinction matters for soft vs hard consonant environments

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Present three Ukrainian words side-by-side with English cognates or near-equivalents — **мама** / **oko** / **sin** — and ask: *"How many syllables?"* Then count by vowels. Learner discovers the rule (one vowel = one syllable) before it's stated. Follow with **молоко** — three О's, three syllables — and contrast with English "milk" (no reduction). The schwa-vs-pure contrast lands viscerally.
- **Best context**: Kitchen/food vocabulary grounds vowels in daily life — каша, сир, їжа, юшка, яблуко are all things learners will encounter in Ukrainian homes and markets. Every word teaches a different vowel.
- **Contrastive pairs**:
  - **И vs І**: сир (cheese) / сік (juice) — same consonant frame, different vowel
  - **Я start vs Я after consonant**: яблуко (2 sounds) / мати→м'яти (softening function)
  - **Ї vs Є**: їжак (always /ji/) / Є (softens, can be /je/ at start or softener after consonant)
  - **О stressed vs unstressed**: молоко — all three О's the same (contrast English "photograph")

- **Textbook model**: kravtsova grade 3 (p. 142) organizes phonetics as звуки і букви → наголос — same sequence this module follows. The наголос section there uses multi-syllable repetition as the primary drill type, which maps to the fill-in syllable-count activity in this plan.

---

## Notes for Content Writing

- Module is pre-verbal (M2): ALL instructions in English. Ukrainian appears only in word/example containers — tables, bullet pattern boxes, blockquote syllable drills.
- The И/І section is the pedagogical core — give it the most care. A native Ukrainian И sounds wrong when Russified (closer to Я/И in Russian); emphasize jaw position and the "no exact English equivalent" flag.
- Ї cultural note (Ukrainian identity symbol) is brief but motivating — include it, don't skip it.
- Minimal pair сир/сір is ideal for И vs І; сір (grey, adjective) is verified via context though it's an adjective form — use it as a listening discrimination example, not a vocabulary target.
- Stress marks (´) are the ONLY phonetic notation allowed at A1 — no IPA, no Latin transliteration.
- Word budget is 1200: 300 words on base vowels is the largest section — stay disciplined.

---

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.
- [MODULE] Hallucinated word "сір" claimed as Ukrainian for "grey". The correct word is "сірий". Replace the сир/сір minimal pair with a verified pair like кит/кіт in both prose and quiz activity.
- [MODULE] Reversed stress marks: яйце́ (singular, final stress) and я́йця (plural, initial stress). Module had them backwards.
- [MODULE] image-to-letter activity asks "What vowel does it start with?" but 4/8 items have consonant answers (Р, М, С). Change title to "What letter does it start with?" or replace items with vowel-initial words.
- [MODULE] Wrong stress: ї́жак should be їжа́к.
- [MODULE] Syllable counting explanation for Ї is nonsensical: "Ї counts as one syllable because the Й part is a consonant". Correct: Ї forms a syllable because it contains the vowel І.

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/vowel-sounds.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
